title: Panic Attacks: What Is Happening and What Helps
subtitle: A reading on the physiology of panic and what the evidence shows about managing it
excerpt: A reading on what is actually happening in the body during a panic attack, how the misinterpretation of those sensations makes it worse, and what the evidence shows about what helps.
sourceNotes: Draws on published cognitive and physiological research on panic disorder, including David Clark's cognitive model of panic (1986) and research on the fight-or-flight response. Framing is original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: Use after a panic episode when you want to understand what was happening, or as preparation if panic attacks are something you experience regularly.

content.text: A panic attack is a rapid activation of the body's threat response in the absence of an actual threat. The symptoms (racing heart, shortness of breath, dizziness, the sense that something is very wrong) are the physiological package the body deploys when it believes danger is present. Understanding what is happening does not make it stop immediately, but it changes what the experience means, which is most of what drives the cycle.
What is happening in the body

The fight-or-flight response is designed to prepare the body for rapid physical action. Adrenaline and cortisol are released. Heart rate increases to pump more blood to the muscles. Breathing quickens to take in more oxygen. Blood is redirected away from digestion and toward the limbs. The result is a collection of strong physical sensations that are perfectly calibrated for outrunning a physical threat, and deeply alarming when there is no visible threat to outrun.
The cycle that makes it worse

Cognitive research on panic identifies a specific mechanism: the misinterpretation of bodily sensations. When the heart rate increases, the person in the panic interprets this as evidence that something is physically wrong: a heart attack, a breakdown, the beginning of something catastrophic. This interpretation is itself alarming, which drives the threat response higher, which intensifies the physical sensations, which confirms the interpretation. The panic feeds itself.

This is why understanding the physiology matters. "My heart is racing because my body thinks it is in danger" is a different interpretation from "my heart is racing, something is wrong with my heart." The first interpretation is accurate and has a known arc. The second drives the spiral.

Slowing the breath is the fastest direct intervention available, because the breath is the one part of the autonomic response you can control voluntarily. A slower out-breath activates the parasympathetic nervous system and begins to counteract the adrenaline response. The technique is simple: breathe in for four counts, hold for two, out for six. The longer exhalation is the active part.

Grounding techniques (physical contact with surfaces, naming what you can see and hear) redirect the attention away from the internal physical sensations and toward the present environment. This interrupts the monitoring loop that sustains the misinterpretation cycle.

Over time, what reduces panic frequency is tolerating the sensations without acting on the interpretation. The more a person learns to sit with the physical experience without catastrophising it, the less the body's alarm system escalates. Avoidance of situations that have triggered panic tends to maintain it; gradual return to those situations, with the skills in place, tends to reduce it.

Draws on David Clark's cognitive model of panic (1986), published research on the physiology of the fight-or-flight response, and evidence-based approaches to panic management. Framing is original to homemade.education.
